nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical coral reefs and lagoons with hard substrate and light sand covering; found on reef slopes, lagoon floors, and rocky outcroppings from the surface to moderate depths

decorPreferences: porous live rock for foot attachment
varied substrate with sandy and rocky areas
open rockwork to allow disc expansion
powerhead guards — anemone will wander and can be shredded by impellers
no sharp surfaces that can damage the column

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; supplements nutrition through active predation. Target-feed weekly with silversides, krill, small pieces of shrimp, clams, marine worms, or other meaty marine foods. Hosting clownfish can provide additional nutrients through waste and mucus exchange. Avoid overfeeding — uneaten food degrades water quality and bloating can be fatal.

goodWith: most other Amphiprion species (14+ documented host relationships)
reef-safe fish that will not nip tentacles
avoidWith: other anemone species — territorial and will sting neighboring anemones
SPS and LPS corals without adequate separation — potent sting damages neighbors
powerheads and circulation pumps without foam guards
angelfish and butterflyfish — tend to nip tentacles
triggers, puffers, and other large predatory fish

summary: The Sebae Anemone is the most prolific clownfish host of any anemone, accommodating 14+ species. It demands intense lighting, stable reef chemistry, and an experienced keeper; its potent sting and wandering habit require careful tank design.
careNotes:
Heteractis crispa (Sebae or Leathery Sea Anemone) is generally considered an advanced-care anemone in the marine hobby. It is notable for hosting the largest number of clownfish species of any anemone — 14 to 15 documented Amphiprion and Premnas species — making it ecologically significant and popular with clownfish keepers.

Taxonomic note: In 2023, taxonomic revision reclassified this species as Radianthus crispa, moving it out of Heteractis. Both names currently appear in hobby literature and retailer listings; Heteractis crispa remains the most commonly used name in aquarium trade and care guides as of 2026. The sibling species Heteractis magnifica (Ritteri Anemone) was similarly reclassified as Radianthus magnifica. See [ritteri-anemone] for comparison.

Key care requirements:

• Lighting: Strong, high-intensity lighting is generally required — typically advised in the range of 200–400 PAR. The anemone tends to wander toward light sources and may climb high in the tank seeking greater illumination. New arrivals should be acclimated gradually over several weeks, starting at lower intensities to prevent bleaching.

• Wandering behavior: Sebae Anemones have been known to roam extensively, particularly in tanks with inadequate lighting. All powerheads and circulation pumps must be covered with foam guards to prevent the anemone from being shredded by impellers. An open aquascape with varied rocky and sandy zones is recommended to allow natural foot placement.

• Foot attachment: The foot anchors to live rock or buried hard substrate, often in crevices or under rock overhangs. Sandy substrate is used for burrowing attachment but not as a sole substrate. Porous live rock tends to be preferred over smooth surfaces.

• Flow: Moderate, intermittent flow is generally recommended. Strong direct laminar flow can stress the anemone; alternating gyre-type or wave-making flow is preferred. Tentacles should gently sway but not stream flat.

• Water quality: Stable, pristine water chemistry is required. Nitrates should generally be kept below 10–20 ppm and phosphates below 0.05–0.1 ppm. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero. Iodine and trace element supplementation has been reported to support health.

• Clownfish symbiosis: The presence of a compatible, actively hosting clownfish (particularly A. clarkii, A. sebae, or A. polymnus — its primary wild hosts) has been reported to improve acclimation and long-term health. A healthy anemone becomes noticeably sticky and will grip firmly to objects placed near it.

• Feeding: Target-feed weekly with small pieces of meaty marine foods. Spot-feeding with tongs or a turkey baster near the oral disc tends to produce best results. Do not overfeed — uneaten prey left in the tentacles leads to water quality issues.

• Acclimation difficulty: Newly imported wild-caught specimens are often bleached (zooxanthellae depleted) and require weeks to months of careful feeding and gradual light acclimation to recover color and health. Captive-acclimated or tank-raised individuals are significantly easier to establish.

• Size in captivity: Oral disc typically reaches 20–50 cm in well-maintained aquaria, with wild specimens occasionally exceeding this. Colony size is influenced by tank size and lighting quality.

• Comparison to Ritteri Anemone (H. magnifica): H. crispa is generally considered somewhat easier to keep than the Ritteri, with a more flexible foot-placement habit (attaches to rock, branching coral, or semi-buried substrate rather than exclusively exposed high-light positions) and reportedly better tolerance for moderate conditions. Both carry potent stings and require high-intensity lighting.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction in captivity is not practiced in the hobby. In the wild, H. crispa reproduces both sexually (broadcast spawning) and asexually via scissiparity (the column divides into two individuals). Asexual fission has been documented in captive specimens under stable, long-term conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Sebae Anemone
primary.notes: Temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, min tank 30 gal (113L), max size 20 in (50cm), care level Difficult, carnivore, temperament Aggressive, reef compatible with caution, lighting High, flow Medium, weekly feeding.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Heteractis crispa (Radianthus crispa)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Stichodactylidae, Order Actiniaria), 2023 reclassification to Radianthus crispa, native range (Red Sea/East Africa to Polynesia, south Japan to Australia/New Caledonia), oral disc 20–50 cm, tentacle length 10–15 cm, depth 0–40 m, hosts 15 anemonefish species, scissiparity reproduction.

additional.name: Reeflings — Heteractis crispa Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 22–26°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, min tank 200L, disc ~50cm, care 'moderate to easy', semi-aggressive. Significantly larger min tank (200L vs 113L); 200L adopted as the more conservative/appropriate value. Temperature lower bound agrees; SG minimum averaged between 1.020 and 1.023 → 1.022.
